text: The patient is positioned supine with arms extended, and the abdomen is prepped and draped. A 5mm trocar is inserted into the left upper quadrant, visualizing all three layers of the abdominal wall. The peritoneum is entered, and CO2 gas is insufflated to achieve pneumoperitoneum at 15 mmHg. The surgeon initially uses a zero-degree scope to verify pneumoperitoneum, then switches to a 30-degree 5mm scope for the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the left inguinal hernia and places the patient in the Trendelenburg position. An incision is made to the right side of the umbilicus, and a 12mm trocar is inserted for the robotic camera. An additional 8mm robotic trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant, and the OptiView trocar is replaced with an 8mm robotic trocar.

The surgeon places a medium-sized Bard 3D Max inguinal hernia mesh designed for the left side into the abdomen, along with two ovicral sutures on SH needles. An outside view shows the trocars positioned before docking the robot, which is then brought over the left hip and docked.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand and a monopolar shear in the right hand to begin the operation by dissecting the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal hernia. The dissection extends laterally onto the transversalis fascia, creating space for the mesh. The epigastric vessels are identified as a landmark, and Cooper's ligament is located medial to these vessels on the pubic tubercle. An interrupted vial suture is placed on Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh. The surgeon then meticulously dissects the hernia sac, ensuring hemostasis to maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon uses monopolar shears for blunt dissection to detach tissue near the epigastric vessels, approaching the internal inguinal ring. The hernia sac is visible, and the surgeon aggressively works on the anterior surface while avoiding injury to the sac. Posteriorly, cord structures come into view as adhesions and preperitoneal fat are pushed off the hernia sac. The surgeon scores and cuts the sac to establish a plane, revealing chord structures underneath. The sac is gradually reduced with careful attention to hemostasis, creating a clean inferior edge for mesh placement in the preperitoneal space. The transversalis fascia is visible laterally.

The surgeon examines the mesh, noting how it retracts into the hernia. The posterior edge of the hernia sac is carefully dissected, with attention to the gonadal vessels and spermatic cord. A window is created to identify the cord structures below, while the anterior creme esterics are managed with energy sources to prevent bleeding. The vas deferens is visible in the inferior position.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens adjacent to the hernia sac. The bleeding cremasteric fibers are cauterized and removed, while the cord structures are carefully pushed down to avoid injury. The iliac vessels are visible beneath the cord structures, and the surgeon proceeds with caution to avoid the femoral and iliac vessels.

The surgeon uses two 5mm needle droppers to perform blunt dissection, creating space for the mesh placement. The mesh is positioned towards the pubic tubercle and adjusted into place. The surgeon then uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture on an SH needle to tack the mesh to the pubic tubercle, ensuring proper placement.

The surgeon uses the shaft of the needle driver to push medial tissue aside for better visualization. A needle driver is used to take a bite of the pubic tubercle and the apex of the mesh, tying a knot while maintaining tissue displacement. The needle driver breaks the sutures by turning them ninety degrees away from each other, demonstrating this technique twice. Three sutures are placed to hold the mesh in position: one at the pubic tubercle, one laterally on the transversalis, and one anteriorly. The top edge of the mesh is positioned beneath the peritoneum closure on the anterior surface.

The surgeon uses a V-lock absorbable barbed suture to close the peritoneum from the medial aspect, ensuring the tissue is cinched down effectively. The running suture technique is employed to close the peritoneal defect, incorporating the hernia sac into the closure with a single bite to secure it.

The surgeon uses a barbed suture to close the peritoneum, pulling the suture laterally and medially to remove slack and cinch it down. The suture holds the peritoneum securely without tearing, and no knots are needed due to the suture's locking mechanism. The peritoneum is closed, the mesh remains intact, and the robot is undocked as the needles are removed and the fascia is closed.
